ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for hashima (Rana chensinensis fallopian tube fat). Mapped to closest frog-product food preparation node. Exact match unavailable in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated hashima entry. A0CKP (amphibians, processed) is the closest defensible anchor. Rock sugar component not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hashima or hasma as a food substance. SNOMED CT coverage of traditional Chinese tonic foods is limited.

main_ingredients: hashima (dried Rana chensinensis fallopian tube fat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of hashima with rock sugar is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one small dessert bowl of the double-boiled sweet soup.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sugars, granulated (rock sugar proxy) and frog legs, raw (hashima protein proxy); composite estimation for prepared hashima dessert soup
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for hashima (Rana chensinensis fallopian tube fat) as a prepared dessert. Nutrient values are estimated using USDA FoodData Central data for frog legs (raw, FDC ID 174438) as the closest approved protein-source proxy for hashima, and USDA data for granulated sugar as the rock sugar proxy. The prepared soup is approximately 85–90% water by weight after double-boiling, which dilutes macros significantly. Input AI-generated values (protein 2.5 g, fat 0.1 g, carbohydrate 11.5 g, energy 55 kcal per 100 g) are broadly consistent with this diluted-soup estimation and are retained with medium confidence. Saturated fat estimated from frog legs USDA profile. Micronutrient values estimated from USDA frog legs and rock sugar data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sucrose and sugar-sweetened beverages — analogous estimate for rock sugar component; University of Sydney GI Database reference for sucrose GI = 65; diluted dessert soup estimated GI ~45
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hashima with rock sugar as a prepared dessert. Hashima itself (fallopian tube fat, primarily protein and collagen) contributes negligible carbohydrate and has a near-zero GI. Rock sugar (sucrose) has a GI of approximately 65 per the University of Sydney GI Database. The prepared soup is highly diluted (~85–90% water), resulting in a low total carbohydrate load per serving. The composite GI is estimated at approximately 45 (low category), consistent with a diluted sucrose-containing dessert soup. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (45 × 11.5 g carbohydrate × 2) / 100 = 10.35, rounded to 10.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (15 mg per 100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Hashima with rock sugar is very low in sodium and fat, making it suitable for individuals managing hypertension; however, portion size should be kept to one bowl to avoid excess sugar intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rock sugar contributes approximately 8.5 g of free sugars per 100 g; low GI (~45) but sugar content warrants monitoring
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume hashima with rock sugar occasionally in small portions; the low GI (~45) limits acute glycemic impact, but the free sugar content from rock sugar should be accounted for within daily carbohydrate targets, and sugar quantity should be reduced or substituted where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (~0.03 g per 100 g); low sodium; no cholesterol data available
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hashima with rock sugar is very low in saturated fat and sodium, posing minimal cardiovascular risk; the primary consideration is the moderate free sugar content from rock sugar, which should be limited in individuals with cardiovascular disease following a low-sugar dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hashima with Rock Sugar (雪蛤糖水, Xuě Há Táng Shuǐ) is a prized traditional Chinese dessert soup made from hashima — the dried fallopian tube fat of the Asiatic grass frog (Rana chensinensis) — double-boiled with rock sugar and water. When rehydrated, hashima expands dramatically into soft, translucent, gelatinous clouds with a delicate, slightly chewy texture and a mild, neutral flavour. The dessert is served warm or at room temperature as a light, sweet soup. It is deeply embedded in traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) culture, where it is valued for its purported 'Yin-nourishing' properties, believed to support skin complexion, respiratory health, and general vitality. Hashima is particularly popular in Guangdong cuisine and among Chinese communities in Singapore and Hong Kong, where it is served at high-end banquets and as a luxury home tonic. Nutritionally, the prepared soup is low in calories (~55 kcal per 100 g), low in fat (0.1 g), and provides modest protein (2.5 g) and carbohydrate (11.5 g) primarily from rock sugar. It has a low glycemic index (~45) due to its high water content and diluted sugar load. It is very low in sodium (15 mg per 100 g), making it suitable for hypertension management. The moderate free sugar content from rock sugar requires monitoring for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Hashima is not vegetarian or vegan as it is an animal-derived product.
common_user_queries: Calories in hashima with rock sugar

confidence_reason: No exact entry for hashima with rock sugar exists in any approved primary nutrient database (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated using USDA FoodData Central frog legs (raw) as the closest approved protein-source proxy and USDA sugar data for the rock sugar component, adjusted for the high water content of the prepared soup. The AI-generated input values are broadly consistent with this estimation and are retained with medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database sucrose reference, adjusted for dilution.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for hashima (Rana chensinensis fallopian tube fat) prepared as a dessert soup are not available in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0 as frog-derived products and sugar-water preparations are not known sources of vitamin D; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central frog legs data showing negligible vitamin D. 3. The input source 'Journal of Food Science and Biotechnology (http://spyswjs.cnjournals.com)' is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved nutrient reference. 4. The input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'vegan', which are incorrect — hashima is an animal-derived product (frog fallopian tube fat) and is neither vegetarian nor vegan; these flags have been corrected to false. 5. The input listed two identical portions; deduplicated to one default serving of 200 g. 6. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct peer-reviewed GI measurement for hashima with rock sugar was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 7. Halal status is uncertain as hashima is derived from frog, which is not universally accepted as halal; is_halal set to false pending authoritative ruling.
